---
title: Building a garden trellis
date: 2024-05-18
tags: [woodworking, gardening, diy]
---

The cucumbers and pole beans needed something stronger than the bamboo tepees I had been using, so I built a pair of A-frame trellises from cedar one-by-twos. Each frame is six feet tall and four feet wide, hinged at the top with a length of salvaged leather strap.

The netting is jute twine woven in a six-inch grid. It is strong enough to hold a full crop of beans through a summer storm and cheap enough to compost at the end of the season rather than untangling it. Stainless steel screws at every crossing of the frame keep the geometry honest over years of weather.

Setting the trellis over a raised bed changed how I plant. Climbing crops go at the base of the frame, low bushy crops at the outer edges, and shade-tolerant greens like lettuce tuck underneath once the vines have filled in. It is the densest and most cheerful corner of the garden.
